title: Jerry Jeff Walker

infobox.value: Ronald Clyde Crosby  (1942-03-16)March 16, 1942  [Oneonta, New York](/wiki/Oneonta,_New_York "Oneonta, New York"), U.S.

(born **Ronald Clyde Crosby**; March 16, 1942 – October 23, 2020)[[3]](#cite_note-Nash-3) was an American [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") and [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") singer-songwriter. He was a leading figure in the [progressive country](/wiki/Progressive_country "Progressive country") and [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country") music movement. He is best known for writing the 1968 song "[Mr. Bojangles](/wiki/Mr._Bojangles_(song) "Mr. Bojangles (song)")".[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Musical artist

**Jerry Jeff Walker** (born **Ronald Clyde Crosby**; March 16, 1942 – October 23, 2020)[[3]](#cite_note-Nash-3) was an American [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") and [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") singer-songwriter. He was a leading figure in the [progressive country](/wiki/Progressive_country "Progressive country") and [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country") music movement. He is best known for writing the 1968 song "[Mr. Bojangles](/wiki/Mr._Bojangles_(song) "Mr. Bojangles (song)")".[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jerry_Jeff_Walker&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Walker was born Ronald Clyde Crosby in [Oneonta, New York](/wiki/Oneonta,_New_York "Oneonta, New York"), on March 16, 1942. His father, Mel, worked as a sports referee and bartender; his mother, Alma (Conrow), was a housewife.[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-5) His maternal grandparents played for square dances in the Oneonta area[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-5) – his grandmother, Jessie Conrow, playing piano, while his grandfather played fiddle. During the late 1950s, Crosby was a member of a local Oneonta teen band called the Tones.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

After high school, Crosby joined the National Guard, but his thirst for adventure led him to go [AWOL](/wiki/AWOL "AWOL"), and he was eventually discharged.[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-5)[[7]](#cite_note-WP_obit-7) He went on to roam the country [busking](/wiki/Busking "Busking") for a living in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") and throughout Texas, Florida, and New York, often accompanied by [H. R. Stoneback](/wiki/H._R._Stoneback "H. R. Stoneback") (a friendship referenced in 1970's *Stoney*).[[8]](#cite_note-8) He first played under the stage name of Jerry Ferris, then Jeff Walker, before amalgamating them into Jerry Jeff Walker and legally [changing his name](/wiki/Name_change "Name change") to that in the late 1960s.[[7]](#cite_note-WP_obit-7)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jerry_Jeff_Walker&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Walker spent his early [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") days in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village") in the mid-1960s.[[9]](#cite_note-Larkin-9) He co-founded a band with Bob Bruno in the late 1960s called [Circus Maximus](/wiki/Circus_Maximus_(American_band) "Circus Maximus (American band)") that put out two albums,[[9]](#cite_note-Larkin-9) one with the popular FM radio hit "Wind", but Bruno's interest in [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") apparently diverged from Walker's interest in folk music.[[9]](#cite_note-Larkin-9) Walker thus resumed his solo career and recorded the seminal 1968 album *Mr. Bojangles* with the help of [David Bromberg](/wiki/David_Bromberg "David Bromberg") and other influential [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") recording artists.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) He settled in [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), in the 1970s, associating mainly with the [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country") scene that included artists such as [Michael Martin Murphey](/wiki/Michael_Martin_Murphey "Michael Martin Murphey"), [Willie Nelson](/wiki/Willie_Nelson "Willie Nelson"), [Guy Clark](/wiki/Guy_Clark "Guy Clark"), [Waylon Jennings](/wiki/Waylon_Jennings "Waylon Jennings"),[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-5) and [Townes Van Zandt](/wiki/Townes_Van_Zandt "Townes Van Zandt").[[12]](#cite_note-12) "Jerry Jeff's train songs" (such as "[Desperados Waiting for a Train](/wiki/Desperados_Waiting_for_a_Train "Desperados Waiting for a Train")") were cited in the lyrics of Jennings and Nelson's 1977 hit song "[Luckenbach, Texas (Back to the Basics of Love)](/wiki/Luckenbach,_Texas_(Back_to_the_Basics_of_Love) "Luckenbach, Texas (Back to the Basics of Love)")".[[13]](#cite_note-13) On September 28, 1974, Walker appeared with [Doug Sahm](/wiki/Doug_Sahm "Doug Sahm") at the Main Hall of [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall").

A string of records for [MCA](/wiki/MCA_Inc. "MCA Inc.") and [Elektra](/wiki/Elektra_Records "Elektra Records") followed Walker's move to Austin,[[9]](#cite_note-Larkin-9) before he gave up on the mainstream music business and formed his own independent record label. Tried & True Music was founded in 1986,[[14]](#cite_note-AP-14) with his wife Susan as president and manager.[[15]](#cite_note-UT-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Susan also founded Goodknight Music as his management company and Tried & True Artists for his bookings.[[15]](#cite_note-UT-15) A series of increasingly autobiographical records followed under the Tried & True imprint, which also sells his autobiography, *Gypsy Songman*.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 2004, Walker released his first DVD of songs from his past performed in an intimate setting in Austin.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Walker married Susan Streit in 1974 in [Travis County, Texas](/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas").[[3]](#cite_note-Nash-3) They had two children: a son, [Django Walker](/wiki/Django_Walker "Django Walker"), who is also a musician, and a daughter Jessie Jane.[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-5) Walker had a retreat on [Ambergris Caye](/wiki/Ambergris_Caye "Ambergris Caye") in Belize, where he recorded his *Cowboy Boots and Bathing Suits* album in 1998.[[19]](#cite_note-19) He also made a guest appearance on [Ramblin' Jack Elliott](/wiki/Ramblin%27_Jack_Elliott "Ramblin' Jack Elliott")'s 1998 album of duets *[Friends of Mine](/wiki/Friends_of_Mine_(Ramblin%27_Jack_Elliott_album) "Friends of Mine (Ramblin' Jack Elliott album)")*,[[20]](#cite_note-20) singing "[He Was a Friend of Mine](/wiki/He_Was_a_Friend_of_Mine "He Was a Friend of Mine")" and [Woody Guthrie](/wiki/Woody_Guthrie "Woody Guthrie")'s "Hard Travelin'".[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Walker recorded songs written by others such as "[L.A. Freeway](/wiki/L.A._Freeway "L.A. Freeway")" ([Guy Clark](/wiki/Guy_Clark "Guy Clark")), "Up Against the Wall Redneck Mother" ([Ray Wylie Hubbard](/wiki/Ray_Wylie_Hubbard "Ray Wylie Hubbard")),[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-5) "[(Looking for) The Heart of Saturday Night](/wiki/(Looking_for)_The_Heart_of_Saturday_Night "(Looking for) The Heart of Saturday Night")" ([Tom Waits](/wiki/Tom_Waits "Tom Waits")),[[23]](#cite_note-23) and "London Homesick Blues" ([Gary P. Nunn](/wiki/Gary_P._Nunn "Gary P. Nunn")).[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-5) He also interpreted the songs of others such as [Rodney Crowell](/wiki/Rodney_Crowell "Rodney Crowell"), [Townes Van Zandt](/wiki/Townes_Van_Zandt "Townes Van Zandt"), [Paul Siebel](/wiki/Paul_Siebel "Paul Siebel"), [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), [Todd Snider](/wiki/Todd_Snider "Todd Snider"), Dave Roberts, and even a [rodeo clown](/wiki/Rodeo_clown "Rodeo clown") named [Billy Jim Baker](/wiki/Billy_Jim_Baker "Billy Jim Baker"). Walker was given the moniker of "the [Jimmy Buffett](/wiki/Jimmy_Buffett "Jimmy Buffett") of [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")".[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Walker was who first drove Jimmy Buffett to [Key West](/wiki/Key_West "Key West") (from Coconut Grove, Florida in a [Packard](/wiki/Packard "Packard")).[[27]](#cite_note-Outsider-27) The two musicians also co-wrote the song "Railroad Lady" while riding the last run of the *[Panama Limited](/wiki/Panama_Limited "Panama Limited")*.[[27]](#cite_note-Outsider-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### "Mr. Bojangles"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jerry_Jeff_Walker&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: \"Mr. Bojangles\"")]

Walker's "[Mr. Bojangles](/wiki/Mr._Bojangles_(song) "Mr. Bojangles (song)")" (1968) is perhaps his best-known and most-often performed song.[[3]](#cite_note-Nash-3) It is about an obscure but talented alcoholic tap-dancing drifter who Walker had met who, when arrested and jailed in New Orleans, insisted on being identified only as "Bojangles".

[Notable recordings of the song](/wiki/Mr._Bojangles_(song)#Recorded_versions "Mr. Bojangles (song)") include a live version by his bandmate Bromberg on his album *[Demon in Disguise](/wiki/Demon_in_Disguise "Demon in Disguise")*, a single by the [Nitty Gritty Dirt Band](/wiki/Nitty_Gritty_Dirt_Band "Nitty Gritty Dirt Band") that charted at number 9 on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") in 1971 (also released on their album *[Uncle Charlie & His Dog Teddy](/wiki/Uncle_Charlie_%26_His_Dog_Teddy "Uncle Charlie & His Dog Teddy")*), and its inclusion in medley on the 1974 debut self-titled album by [Jim Stafford](/wiki/Jim_Stafford "Jim Stafford").

## Later years and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jerry_Jeff_Walker&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Later years and death")]

Walker had an annual birthday celebration in Austin at the [Paramount Theatre](/wiki/Paramount_Theatre_(Austin,_Texas) "Paramount Theatre (Austin, Texas)") and at [Gruene Hall](/wiki/Gruene_Hall "Gruene Hall") in [Gruene, Texas](/wiki/Gruene,_Texas "Gruene, Texas").[[3]](#cite_note-Nash-3) The party brought some of the biggest names in country music out for a night of [picking](/wiki/Guitar_picking "Guitar picking") and swapping stories.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

He died from throat cancer on October 23, 2020, at a hospital in [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), at the age of 78.[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_obit-5)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Blackstock_2020-31)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jerry_Jeff_Walker&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** *Contrary to Ordinary* also peaked at No. 99 on the *[RPM](/wiki/RPM_(magazine) "RPM (magazine)")* Top Albums chart in Canada.[[37]](#cite_note-RPM-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** "Mr. Bojangles" also peaked at No. 51 on the *[RPM](/wiki/RPM_(magazine) "RPM (magazine)")* Top Singles chart in Canada.[[37]](#cite_note-RPM-37)[[41]](#cite_note-42)
